content: Hey Saturday, I just started David Chen’s online financial modeling course on 2026-02-03, and I’m trying to build my first 3-statement model in Excel. I’m a bit lost on how to link the income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement cleanly.

Nice—David Chen’s course is a solid one for this. Start by building each statement in separate sections, then link Net Income from the income statement into retained earnings on the balance sheet and into cash from operations on the cash flow statement. Finally, make sure cash on the cash flow statement flows back to the balance sheet so the model balances each period.
That helps. He also switched from VLOOKUP to INDEX-MATCH for assumptions, like revenue growth by scenario. Why is INDEX-MATCH better in practice?

INDEX-MATCH is more flexible because it can look left or right, unlike VLOOKUP. It’s also safer when columns get inserted, since you’re not relying on a hard-coded column number. In bigger models, that makes scenario assumptions way less fragile.
Got it. I hit a circular reference when calculating interest expense because debt depends on cash flow, and cash flow depends on interest. David mentioned iterative calc, but I’m nervous about it.

That’s a common circularity in 3-statement models, so you’re not doing anything wrong. You can enable iterative calculation with tight settings (like max change 0.01 or lower) and keep the interest schedule simple so it converges fast. Also add a check line to confirm the model still balances after each run.

Awesome. Last thing: I want to use this for my own stock analysis—thinking of modeling Nvidia and doing a DCF with a sensitivity table for WACC and terminal growth. Any practical tips before I trust the valuation?

Great use case—build a 2-way sensitivity table (e.g., WACC 8–12% and terminal growth 2–4%) so you see valuation ranges, not just one point estimate. Cross-check your implied multiples and margins against peers to sanity-check outputs. If the DCF says something extreme, it usually means one assumption is doing too much work.
